title: feat: show signers info in skopeo inspect
body:
If a remote image being inspected has signatures available, show them in
the inspect output under "Signers". Attempt to display the verified key
fingerprints (if a matching public key is available locally), otherwise
fallback to showing the unverified key IDs.

comments.body: Popping this up just as a first draft proposal, as I needed the functionality recently so it was quick to code it up like this. However, looking for feedback on the viability of the change being accepted into skopeo in general, and if there’s any thoughts on how the signer info should be formatted etc.

@mtrmac thanks — everything you say makes perfect sense and I agree that the current wording and possibly even the inclusion of any key reference at all in the output could inadvertently give a false confidence to a casual observer that the image is trustworthy.

Really the simplistic use case was just to provide a convenient way to determine:

1. does a given image on a given registry (supporting the `/extensions/v2/<namespace>/<name>/signatures/<digest>` extension) have one or more signatures attached and available for verification?
2. if so, what gpg fingerprint needs to be available and locally trusted in order to verify that the remote signature is genuine

> 1. does a given image on a given registry (supporting the /extensions/v2/<namespace>/<name>/signatures/<digest> extension) have one or more signatures attached and available for verification?

Makes sense; including a count of signatures does seem useful (just to verify that the signatures have been copied — especially the sigstore mechanism can easily lead to non-obvious failures), and probably safe enough.

> 2. if so, what gpg fingerprint needs to be available and locally trusted in order to verify that the remote signature is genuine

That’s, in general, the wrong way around — the user should know who is expected to sign a particular image, and only accept that key or set of keys. “Where do I get a key so that this image can be verified”, if it leads to a key server lookup where anyone can upload keys, provides no security. (Again, specific use cases like build systems may need this information for more reasonable purposes. And I suppose some organizations may want to have a delegation/CA system; ideally that would be best handled in the policy.json enforcement mechanism, although no such code currently exists.)
@dnwe Are you still working on this?
@rhatdan I’m happy to close this for now and can always open a different PR based on the discussion above.
